title: Anti-Money Laundering Regulation (EU) 2024/1624 — AMLR
summary: The AMLR is the first directly applicable EU anti-money laundering regulation, replacing the patchwork of national transpositions of AML directives. It introduces harmonised CDD requirements across all member states, enhanced transaction monitoring obligations, mandatory UBO verification, and a €10,000 limit on cash payments. For fintechs, it materially increases the standard for remote onboarding, expands screening requirements, and introduces stricter controls on high-risk customer segments. Unlike directives, this regulation requires no national transposition and will apply uniformly from July 2027.
business_impact: Materially increases compliance overhead for all payment and e-money institutions. Remote onboarding flows must be redesigned to meet enhanced CDD standards. Product restrictions apply to anonymous prepaid instruments above €150. High-risk customer segments require additional controls that will affect conversion rates and cost-to-serve. Restrictions on correspondent relationships tighten operating model options.
operational_impact: Requires review and probable redesign of onboarding workflows, enhanced transaction monitoring logic, expanded AML policy documentation, and additional compliance headcount. Systems capable of real-time screening against updated sanctions and PEP lists become mandatory. Annual risk assessment documentation must be formalised and auditable.
recommendations: Initiate gap analysis against AMLR obligations by Q3 2025. Prioritise onboarding flow review and transaction monitoring uplift. Assess whether outsourced KYC vendors meet the new technical standards. Plan compliance headcount increase. Review prepaid product architecture against anonymous instrument restrictions.
obligations: Enhanced CDD for all customers above defined risk thresholds
Real-time sanctions and PEP screening at onboarding and ongoing
Mandatory UBO verification for all corporate customers
Transaction monitoring with documented risk-based logic
Prohibition on anonymous prepaid instruments above €150
Annual AML risk assessment with board-level sign-off
Documented customer risk scoring methodology

title: Anti-Money Laundering Directive (EU) 2024/1640 — AMLD6
summary: AMLD6 is the sixth Anti-Money Laundering Directive, passed alongside the AMLR. It establishes the supervisory architecture for AML enforcement, expands the list of obliged entities, and sets out the framework for the new EU Anti-Money Laundering Authority (AMLA). Member states must transpose by July 2027. For fintechs, it means heightened supervisory scrutiny, expanded obligations for crypto asset service providers, and more structured internal governance requirements.
business_impact: Increases supervisory expectations for internal governance, risk committees, and AML officer responsibilities. Obliged entities must demonstrate a more structured three-lines-of-defence model. Crypto service providers face explicit inclusion in the directive scope with corresponding oversight obligations.
operational_impact: Requires formalisation of AML governance structures, escalation procedures, and board-level reporting. Internal audit functions must cover AML with documented findings and remediation tracking. Training programmes must be structured and evidence-based.
recommendations: Map current governance structure against AMLD6 requirements by Q4 2025. Upgrade MLRO reporting lines and board engagement on AML matters. Document three-lines-of-defence model with AML coverage. Review training programme adequacy.
obligations: Structured AML governance with board-level oversight
Documented three-lines-of-defence model covering AML
MLRO with appropriate seniority and resources
Annual AML training for all relevant staff
Internal audit coverage of AML function
Supervisory cooperation and information sharing protocols

title: AMLA Regulation (EU) 2024/1620 — Establishment of EU AML Authority
summary: The AMLA Regulation establishes the EU Anti-Money Laundering Authority, a new centralised supervisor with direct oversight powers over selected high-risk cross-border financial institutions from 2027. AMLA will also coordinate national supervisors and set binding technical standards. For fintechs operating cross-border, this introduces the possibility of direct AMLA supervision and a more harmonised but also more intensive supervisory regime.
business_impact: Fintechs with cross-border operations above defined thresholds may fall under direct AMLA supervision, meaning heightened scrutiny and EU-level enforcement. All firms will face new binding technical standards issued by AMLA that may require systems and process changes.
operational_impact: Compliance teams must monitor AMLA technical standard issuances closely. Firms near the direct supervision threshold should prepare for supervisory engagement at EU level. Internal compliance frameworks must align with AMLA-issued guidance as it is published.
recommendations: Assign monitoring responsibility for AMLA technical standard publications from H2 2025. Assess whether the firm is likely to fall within direct supervision scope. Engage with trade associations participating in AMLA consultation processes.
obligations: Compliance with AMLA-issued binding technical standards
Cooperation with AMLA supervisory requests if in-scope
Adoption of standardised AML reporting formats as specified by AMLA
Participation in joint supervisory teams if designated

title: Transfer of Funds Regulation (EU) 2023/1113 — Travel Rule Extension to Crypto
summary: The revised Transfer of Funds Regulation extends the FATF Travel Rule to crypto asset transfers, requiring crypto asset service providers (CASPs) to collect and transmit originator and beneficiary information with every transfer, regardless of value. This creates a significant operational and systems challenge for VASPs, requiring integration with VASP-to-VASP messaging protocols, unhosted wallet verification procedures, and new screening workflows.
business_impact: Crypto transfers below previously exempt thresholds now carry full information requirements. Unhosted wallet transactions require enhanced due diligence and may need to be restricted for certain customer segments. Non-compliant VASPs face transaction blocking obligations. Compliance with counterparty VASPs' travel rule implementations becomes operationally necessary.
operational_impact: Requires integration with a travel rule solution provider, implementation of unhosted wallet verification procedures, and redesign of crypto transfer workflows. Transaction monitoring must incorporate travel rule compliance checks. VASP counterparty onboarding processes must be formalised.
recommendations: Select and integrate a travel rule compliance solution immediately. Implement unhosted wallet risk assessment procedures. Review and update terms of service for crypto transfer products. Document VASP counterparty due diligence framework.
obligations: Collect and transmit originator and beneficiary data for all crypto transfers
Implement unhosted wallet verification for transfers above risk thresholds
Screen counterparty VASPs before processing transfers
Block transfers where required information cannot be obtained
Maintain records of travel rule compliance for five years

title: Markets in Crypto-Assets Regulation (EU) 2023/1114 — MiCA AML Provisions
summary: MiCA introduces a comprehensive licensing and conduct framework for crypto asset service providers in the EU. Its AML provisions require CASPs to apply CDD requirements equivalent to traditional financial institutions, implement transaction monitoring, and report suspicious activity. CASPs must be authorised by national competent authorities and are subject to ongoing supervisory oversight. The regulation is fully applicable from December 2024.
business_impact: Unlicensed CASPs must obtain authorisation or cease operations. Licensed CASPs face compliance costs equivalent to an EMI for AML purposes. Enhanced KYC requirements for crypto onboarding will increase friction and cost-to-serve, particularly for retail segments.
operational_impact: Requires CASP licence application if not already authorised, full KYC programme implementation, transaction monitoring capability, suspicious activity reporting infrastructure, and ongoing compliance with supervisory expectations. Governance and internal controls must meet the same standards as regulated payment institutions.
recommendations: Confirm CASP authorisation status and timeline. Implement KYC and AML programme meeting MiCA standards. Establish SAR/STR filing capability with local FIU. Engage compliance counsel on authorisation requirements if not yet licensed.
obligations: CASP authorisation from national competent authority
Full CDD/KYC programme equivalent to EMI standards
Transaction monitoring with risk-based logic
Suspicious transaction reporting to national FIU
Ongoing supervisory reporting to competent authority
Minimum capital and organisational requirements

title: EBA Guidelines on ML/TF Risk Factors — EBA/GL/2021/02 (Revised)
summary: The revised EBA Guidelines on ML/TF risk factors update the risk-based approach to CDD for all financial institutions. They provide specific guidance on customer risk scoring, product risk assessment, and geographic risk factors. For fintechs, the guidelines introduce specific expectations for digital onboarding risk assessment, remote customer relationships, and the use of technology-based CDD tools. National competent authorities are expected to supervise against these guidelines.
business_impact: Firms without a documented and operationally embedded risk-based approach face supervisory risk. Digital-first customer relationships require explicit risk justification. Reliance on automated screening tools must be documented with human oversight controls.
operational_impact: Customer risk scoring methodology must be documented and reviewed at defined intervals. Product and channel risk assessments must be updated to reflect the guidelines. Onboarding decisions must be traceable to the documented risk framework.
recommendations: Review customer risk scoring model against EBA guidelines. Document product and channel risk assessments. Ensure AML policy references EBA guidance explicitly. Schedule annual review of risk framework against supervisory expectations.
obligations: Documented customer risk scoring methodology with regular review
Product and channel risk assessments aligned with EBA guidance
Risk-based CDD calibration with documented rationale
Human oversight controls on automated screening decisions
Geographic risk factor integration in customer risk model

title: EBA Guidelines on Remote Customer Onboarding — EBA/GL/2022/15
summary: These guidelines set out requirements for the use of digital identity verification and remote customer onboarding tools. They specify the technical and procedural standards for liveness detection, document verification, and biometric matching. Firms must demonstrate that remote onboarding solutions meet defined accuracy and security thresholds. For fintechs relying heavily on automated digital onboarding, these guidelines directly affect vendor selection, monitoring, and internal control requirements.
business_impact: Automated onboarding solutions must be validated against EBA technical standards. Vendors not meeting required accuracy thresholds create compliance risk. Conversion impacts are possible if enhanced liveness detection or human review steps are required. Documentation and audit trail requirements add operational overhead.
operational_impact: KYC technology vendors must be assessed against EBA standards. Internal controls must include monitoring of false acceptance and false rejection rates. Human review escalation procedures must be documented and tested. Regular vendor performance reviews must be conducted and evidenced.
recommendations: Assess current onboarding solution against EBA/GL/2022/15 technical requirements. Request vendor compliance attestation. Implement monitoring of key accuracy metrics. Document escalation procedures for failed automated checks. Review onboarding audit trail completeness.
obligations: Validation of remote onboarding solution against EBA technical thresholds
Monitoring of false acceptance and false rejection rates
Documented escalation procedures for automated check failures
Regular vendor performance review with documented findings
Audit trail for all remote onboarding decisions

title: KNF AML/CFT Supervisory Priorities and Inspection Framework 2025
summary: KNF has published its AML/CFT supervisory priorities for 2025, identifying transaction monitoring quality, sanctions screening adequacy, and remote onboarding controls as the top inspection themes for payment institutions and e-money institutions in Poland. KNF has signalled increased inspection intensity following enforcement actions in 2024 and expects firms to demonstrate a fully documented and operationally embedded risk-based approach. Administrative fines for material AML deficiencies have increased significantly.
business_impact: KNF-licensed fintechs face heightened inspection risk in 2025. Firms with material gaps in transaction monitoring or sanctions screening face administrative action. Reputational risk from KNF enforcement actions is elevated given increased public reporting of sanctions.
operational_impact: AML programmes must be reviewed against KNF inspection expectations. Transaction monitoring models must be documented and tested. Sanctions screening coverage and refresh frequency must be demonstrable. Remote onboarding controls must meet both EBA and KNF standards.
recommendations: Conduct internal AML health check against KNF 2025 priorities by Q2 2025. Test transaction monitoring alerting thresholds and document outcomes. Review sanctions screening vendor coverage and update frequency. Prepare onboarding controls documentation for potential KNF review.
obligations: Documented and tested transaction monitoring model
Sanctions screening with demonstrable coverage and refresh rates
Remote onboarding controls meeting EBA/GL/2022/15
AML risk assessment reviewed and approved by board
MLRO report to management body at minimum quarterly

title: Polish AML Act Amendment — Ustawa AML 2023 Update
summary: The 2023 amendment to the Polish AML Act implements outstanding AMLD transposition requirements and introduces several national-level additions including expanded beneficial ownership reporting obligations, enhanced requirements for politically exposed persons, and updated thresholds for simplified due diligence. Polish-licensed fintechs must ensure their AML programmes reflect the updated national requirements, which in several areas go beyond the EU directive minimum.
business_impact: Polish-licensed fintechs must update AML policies to reflect the 2023 amendment. Enhanced PEP screening and UBO verification requirements increase onboarding complexity. Updated simplified due diligence thresholds may affect low-risk customer handling procedures.
operational_impact: AML policy and procedures must be updated to reflect the 2023 requirements. PEP screening logic must be reviewed against updated definitions. UBO verification procedures for corporate customers must be strengthened. Staff training must be updated to reflect legislative changes.
recommendations: Update AML policy documentation to reflect the 2023 amendment. Review PEP screening rules against updated Polish definitions. Update UBO verification procedures and customer risk scoring. Deliver legislative update training to compliance staff.
obligations: Updated beneficial ownership verification procedures
Enhanced PEP identification aligned with 2023 definitions
Revised simplified due diligence eligibility criteria
Updated AML policy documentation reflecting legislative changes
Staff training on 2023 amendment changes

title: EBA Guidelines on Internal Governance and AML Outsourcing Controls
summary: These guidelines address the use of third-party providers for KYC and AML functions, including outsourced identity verification, transaction screening, and compliance monitoring. Institutions remain responsible for outcomes even when functions are outsourced and must demonstrate oversight, performance monitoring, and contractual controls over third-party AML service providers. The guidelines respond to growing fintech reliance on RegTech vendors for core compliance functions.
business_impact: Fintechs outsourcing KYC or AML functions to third parties must implement demonstrable vendor oversight programmes. Contractual arrangements must include performance metrics, audit rights, and termination provisions. Reliance on vendor certifications is insufficient without internal monitoring.
operational_impact: Third-party AML vendor contracts must be reviewed and updated. Performance monitoring frameworks must be implemented. Annual vendor assessments must be documented. Contingency plans for vendor failure must exist.
recommendations: Review all AML/KYC vendor contracts against EBA outsourcing requirements. Implement vendor performance monitoring dashboards. Conduct and document annual vendor assessments. Ensure contracts include audit rights and exit provisions.
obligations: Documented vendor oversight programme for all outsourced AML functions
Contractual performance metrics and audit rights for AML vendors
Annual documented third-party AML vendor assessment
Contingency plan for critical AML vendor failure
Internal monitoring of outsourced KYC/AML performance
